meta.title: 3.5 Getting Data In
meta.description: All the ways to give Claude information: @-mentions, images, piping, system prompts, and adding directories.

blocks.prompt: Think about the different types of information you work with: documents, spreadsheets, images, websites, emails, databases. For each one, how would you get that information into Claude Code? Some are obvious. Others might surprise you.

blocks.content:
## Every way to get information into Claude

Module 2.5 introduced the basics: @file mentions, image paste, piping, and system prompt files. This module is the comprehensive reference. Bookmark it and come back when you need a reminder.

## Method 1: @-mentions (file references)

Type `@` followed by a file path to include a file's contents directly in your prompt.

```
Summarize the key points in @reports/quarterly-review.md
```

Claude Code shows autocomplete suggestions as you type. The file content is loaded into your prompt immediately — Claude does not need to use a tool call to read it.

**Tips:**
- You can @-mention multiple files: `Compare @v1.md and @v2.md`
- Works with any text file: markdown, CSV, JSON, plain text, code
- The file must be within Claude Code's working directory or added directories
- For very large files, Claude may only receive a portion — consider mentioning specific sections

## Method 2: Image paste (Ctrl+V / Cmd+V)

Copy any image to your clipboard and paste it into Claude Code.

**What works:**
- Screenshots of anything on your screen
- Photos from your camera (copy from photo viewer)
- Diagrams, charts, mockups
- Whiteboard photos
- Screenshots of error messages
- Tables from PDFs that are hard to copy as text

**Practical examples:**
- Paste a screenshot of a spreadsheet: "Extract this data into a CSV file"
- Paste a UI mockup: "Describe what this interface shows and suggest improvements"
- Paste a chart: "What trends do you see in this chart? Summarize the key takeaways."
- Paste a handwritten note: "Transcribe this and format it as a clean to-do list"

## Method 3: Piping from the shell

Pipe data from any command directly into Claude:

```bash
# Quick transforms
cat notes.txt | claude -p "Convert these notes to action items"

# Chain with other commands
curl -s https://api.example.com/data | claude -p "Summarize this JSON"

# Clipboard contents
pbpaste | claude -p "Rewrite this email to be more concise"  # macOS
xclip -o | claude -p "Rewrite this email to be more concise"  # Linux

# Command output
ls -la | claude -p "Which of these files was modified most recently?"

# Interactive session with piped context
cat project-spec.md | claude "Help me estimate the timeline for this project"
```

The `-p` flag means print mode — Claude processes the input, prints a response, and exits. Without `-p`, you start an interactive session with the piped content as initial context.

**When piping shines:** Quick one-off transformations, processing command output, building automated workflows, and working with data from APIs or other tools.

## Method 4: System prompt files

```bash
claude --system-prompt-file brand-guidelines.txt "Write the weekly newsletter"
```

The system prompt file contains persistent instructions that apply to everything in the session. It is loaded before your prompt and shapes all of Claude's responses.

**Good uses for system prompt files:**
- Brand voice and style guidelines
- Company-specific terminology
- Output format requirements
- Role descriptions ("You are a financial analyst reviewing...")
- Rules that apply across many tasks

You can create different system prompt files for different types of work: one for writing, one for data analysis, one for project management.

## Method 5: Adding directories with /add-dir

By default, Claude Code can access files in the directory where you launched it. Use `/add-dir` to expand its reach:

```
/add-dir ~/Documents/project-resources
```

Now Claude can read, search, and reference files in that directory too. This is useful when:
- Your task involves files spread across multiple locations
- You want Claude to reference a shared resource folder
- You are working on a project that depends on files in another project

## Method 6: Let Claude find it

Sometimes the best input method is no input method. If the information exists somewhere in your project, you can just describe what you need:

> Find all files in this project that contain pricing information and create a summary.

Claude will use Glob and Grep to locate the files, Read to examine them, and then produce the summary. You do not need to know where the files are — that is Claude's job.

**Use this when:**
- You do not know exactly which file contains the information
- The information is spread across many files
- You want Claude to be thorough rather than limited to files you thought of

## Choosing the right method — quick reference

| You have... | Use... |
| :-- | :-- |
| A specific file you want Claude to read | @file mention |
| An image, screenshot, or photo | Ctrl+V paste |
| Output from another command | Pipe with `|` |
| Text on your clipboard | Pipe with `pbpaste |` or `xclip -o |` |
| Persistent rules for a type of work | `--system-prompt-file` |
| Files in another directory | `/add-dir` |
| Information somewhere in your project | Just describe it and let Claude search |
| A URL you want Claude to read | Pipe with `curl -s URL |` |

blocks.question: You have a PDF report, a screenshot of a chart from that report, and notes from a meeting about the report — all in different locations. You want Claude to synthesize all three into an executive summary. How would you get all this information to Claude?
blocks.answer: Use multiple input methods in one session: **@mention** the PDF report (Claude can read PDFs), **Ctrl+V paste** the chart screenshot, and either **@mention** the meeting notes file or **describe** what the meeting notes contain if they are in your head. Your prompt might look like: 'Create an executive summary that combines the findings from @reports/q1.pdf, this chart [paste image], and these meeting notes @notes/q1-meeting.md. Focus on the three most actionable insights.' Claude handles all three input types in a single interaction.

blocks.title: Try a piping workflow. Pick something from your clipboard or a file on your system and pipe it into Claude with a transformation request. For example: 'pbpaste | claude -p "Rewrite this to be more concise"' (macOS) or 'xclip -o | claude -p "Rewrite this to be more concise"' (Linux). Try at least one pipe-based command and one @-mention in the same day.
blocks.solution: Piping should feel fast and direct — you get a result without starting an interactive session. This is ideal for quick transformations. Compare it to @-mentions, which are better for interactive work where you might ask follow-up questions. The goal is to have both tools in your repertoire and choose based on the situation: piping for one-shot transforms, @-mentions for interactive exploration.

questions: What types of information do you most often need to share with Claude? Which input method maps best to each type?
Have you been manually copy-pasting information that could be @-mentioned or piped instead? What would change if you switched?
Think about a recurring task you do with Claude. Would a system prompt file save you from repeating the same instructions every time?

blocks.content: Claude Code has six ways to receive information: @-mentions for specific files, Ctrl+V for images, piping for shell data and quick transforms, --system-prompt-file for persistent rules, /add-dir for expanding scope, and letting Claude search for it. Choose the method that matches your data source. Combine methods for complex tasks.

blocks.prompt: Getting data in is half the equation. The next module covers getting results out — copying output, exporting conversations, structured output formats, and using print mode for quick answers that feed into other tools.
